MBECS Fibrinogen

Gabriela Puerres

2025-07-19

Evaluation and correction of batch effects in microbiome data-sets

  • Package ‘MBECS’ Version 1.12.0
  • Description: The Microbiome Batch Effect Correction Suite (MBECS) provides a set of functions to evaluate and mitigate unwated noise due to processing in batches. To that end it incorporates a host of batch correcting algorithms (BECA) from various packages. In addition it offers a correction and reporting pipeline that provides a preliminary look at the characteristics of a data-set before and after correcting for batch effects.

References:

  • Olbrich M (2025). MBECS: Evaluation and correction of batch effects in microbiome data-sets. doi:10.18129/B9.bioc.MBECS, R package version 1.12.0, https://bioconductor.org/packages/MBECS.

URLS

  • GitHub: https://github.com/rmolbrich/MBECS
  • MBECS workflow: https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/vignettes/MBECS/inst/doc/mbecs_vignette.html
  • Documentation: https://www.bioconductor.org/packages/release/bioc/html/MBECS.html

Libraries

library(MBECS) 
library (phyloseq)
library(dplyr)
library(Biostrings)

The user can utilize objects of class phyloseq or a list object that contains an abundance table as well as meta data. The package contains a dummy data-set of artificially generated data to illustrate this process. To start an analysis, the user requires the mbecProcessInput() function.

DATA:

# Phyloseq object 
ps <- readRDS("~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/ps.st.decontam.rds")
ps <- subset_samples(ps, Sample_Type == "Feces")
ps
## phyloseq-class experiment-level object
## otu_table()   OTU Table:         [ 21727 taxa and 142 samples ]
## sample_data() Sample Data:       [ 142 samples by 68 sample variables ]
## tax_table()   Taxonomy Table:    [ 21727 taxa by 7 taxonomic ranks ]
# As the names of the sequences in the OTU table is the sequence itself.A name as ASVxx will be assigned:
old_seqs <- taxa_names(ps)      
new_ids  <- paste0("ASV", seq_along(old_seqs))
taxa_names(ps) <- new_ids

1. Data from Phyloseq Object

#The dummy.ps object contains the same data as dummy.list, but it is of class phyloseq. Create an MbecData object from phyloseq input.

#The optional argument required.col may be used to ensure that all covariate columns that should be there are available. For the dummy-data these are “group”, “batch” and “replicate”.

mbec.obj <- mbecProcessInput(ps)
## No 'sID' column present, creating from rownames now.
str(mbec.obj)
## Formal class 'MbecData' [package "MBECS"] with 9 slots
##   ..@ assessments: list()
##   ..@ corrections: list()
##   ..@ tss        : num [1, 1] 0
##   ..@ clr        : num [1, 1] 0
##   ..@ otu_table  :Formal class 'otu_table' [package "phyloseq"] with 2 slots
##   .. .. ..@ .Data        : num [1:142, 1:21727] 0 0 0 0 1978 ...
##   .. .. .. ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
##   .. .. .. .. ..$ : chr [1:142] "1165D1LM" "1165D3LM" "1179D1LM" "1179D3LM" ...
##   .. .. .. .. ..$ : chr [1:21727] "ASV1" "ASV2" "ASV3" "ASV4" ...
##   .. .. ..@ taxa_are_rows: logi FALSE
##   .. .. ..$ dim     : int [1:2] 142 21727
##   .. .. ..$ dimnames:List of 2
##   .. .. .. ..$ : chr [1:142] "1165D1LM" "1165D3LM" "1179D1LM" "1179D3LM" ...
##   .. .. .. ..$ : chr [1:21727] "ASV1" "ASV2" "ASV3" "ASV4" ...
##   ..@ tax_table  :Formal class 'taxonomyTable' [package "phyloseq"] with 1 slot
##   .. .. ..@ .Data: chr [1:21727, 1:7] "Bacteria" "Bacteria" "Bacteria" "Bacteria" ...
##   .. .. .. ..- attr(*, "dimnames")=List of 2
##   .. .. .. .. ..$ : chr [1:21727] "ASV1" "ASV2" "ASV3" "ASV4" ...

## Formal class 'sample_data' [package "phyloseq"] with 4 slots

##   .. .. ..@ row.names: chr [1:142] "1165D1LM" "1165D3LM" "1179D1LM" "1179D3LM" ...
##   .. .. ..@ .S3Class : chr "data.frame"
##   ..@ phy_tree   : NULL
##   ..@ refseq     : NULL

2. Normalization

The most common normalizing transformations in microbiome analysis are total sum scaling (TSS) and centered log-ratio transformation (CLR).

Hence, the MBECS package offers these two methods. The resulting matrices will be stored in their respective slots (tss, clr) in the MbecData object, while the original abundance table will remain unchanged.

Use mbecTransform() to apply total sum scaling to the data. Apply centered log-ratio transformation to the data. Due to the sparse nature of compositional microbiome data, the parameter offset may be used to add a small offset to the abundance matrix in order to facilitate the CLR transformation.

#> Set tss-transformed counts.
mbec.obj  <- mbecTransform(mbec.obj, method = "tss")
## Set tss-transformed counts.
#> Set clr-transformed counts.
mbec.obj  <- mbecTransform(mbec.obj, method = "clr", offset = 0.0001)

3. Preliminary Report

The function mbecReportPrelim() will provide the user with an overview of experimental setup and the significance of the batch effect. To that end it is required to declare the covariates that are related to batch effect and group effect respectively. In addition it provides the option to select the abundance table to use here. The CLR transformed abundances are the default and the function will calculate them if they are not present in the input. Technically, the user can start the analysis at this point because the function incorporates the functionality of the aforementioned processing functions.

mbecReportPrelim(input.obj=mbec.obj ,
                 model.vars=c("ExtractionDate",
                              "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"), 
                 type="clr")

##   |                                                                                        |                                                                                |   0%  |                                                                                        |========================================                                        |  50%  |                                                                                        |================================================================================| 100%
## [1] "ExtractionDate"
## [1] "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"
##   |                                                                 |                                                         |   0%  |                                                                 |..                                                       |   4%               |                                                                 |.....                                                    |   8% [SETUP]       |                                                                 |.......                                                  |  12%               |                                                                 |.........                                                |  16% [COVARIATES]  |                                                                 |...........                                              |  20%               |                                                                 |..............                                           |  24% [MOSAIC]
##   |                                                                 |................                                         |  28%               |                                                                 |..................                                       |  32% [PCA]
##   |                                                                 |.....................                                    |  36%               |                                                                 |.......................                                  |  40% [RLE]
##   |                                                                 |.........................                                |  44%               |                                                                 |...........................                              |  48% [HEAT]        |                                                                 |..............................                           |  52%               |                                                                 |................................                         |  56% [BOX]
##   |                                                                 |..................................                       |  60%               |                                                                 |....................................                     |  64% [LINMOD]
##   |                                                                 |.......................................                  |  68%               |                                                                 |.........................................                |  72% [LINMIXMOD]
##   |                                                                 |...........................................              |  76%               |                                                                 |..............................................           |  80% [RDA]
##   |                                                                 |................................................         |  84%               |                                                                 |..................................................       |  88% [PVCA]
##   |                                                                 |....................................................     |  92%               |                                                                 |.......................................................  |  96% [SCOEF]
##   |                                                                 |.........................................................| 100%             
## /usr/lib/rstudio-server/bin/quarto/bin/tools/x86_64/pandoc +RTS -K512m -RTS mbecReport_prelim.knit.md --to html4 --from markdown+autolink_bare_uris+tex_math_single_backslash --output /home/dpuerres/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/mbecReport_prelim.html --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/pagebreak.lua --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/latex-div.lua --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/table-classes.lua --embed-resources --standalone --variable bs3=TRUE --section-divs --table-of-contents --toc-depth 3 --variable toc_float=1 --variable toc_selectors=h1,h2,h3 --variable toc_collapsed=1 --variable toc_smooth_scroll=1 --variable toc_print=1 --template /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmd/h/default.html --highlight-style kate --variable theme=cerulean --mathjax --variable 'mathjax-url=https://mathjax.rstudio.com/latest/MathJax.js?config=TeX-AMS-MML_HTMLorMML' --include-in-header /tmp/RtmpZlcG9k/rmarkdown-str2888a5495967f.html

The parameter model.vars is a character vector with two elements. The first denotes the covariate column that describes the batch effect and the second one should be used for the presumed biological effect of interest, e.g., the group effect in case/control studies. The type parameter selects which abundance table is to be used “otu”, “clr”, “tss” .

4. Corrections (Select the type!)

The package acts as a wrapper for six different batch effect correcting algorithms (BECA).

  1. Remove Unwanted Variation 3 (ruv3)
  2. Batch Mean Centering (bmc)
  3. ComBat (bat)
  4. Remove Batch Effect (rbe)
  5. Percentile Normalization (pn)
  6. Support Vector Decomposition (svd)

The user has the choice between two functions mbecCorrection() and mbecRunCorrections(), the latter one acts as a wrapper that can apply multiple correction methods in a single run. If the input to mbecRunCorrections() is missing CLR and TSS transformed abundance matrices, they will be created with default settings, i.e., offsets for zero values will be set to 1/#features.

mbec.obj  <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj , model.vars=c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"), 
                            method = "bat", type = "clr")
## Applying ComBat (sva) for batch-correction.
## Found6batches
## Adjusting for1covariate(s) or covariate level(s)
## Standardizing Data across genes
## Fitting L/S model and finding priors
## Finding nonparametric adjustments
## Adjusting the Data
mbec.obj <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj, model.vars = c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"),
                               method = "ruv3", type = "clr")
## No negative control features provided.
##                 Using pseudo-negative controls.
## Applying Remove Unwanted Variantion v3 (RUV-III).
## Warning in mbecRUV3(input.obj, model.vars, type = eval(type), nc.features): No
## technical replicates found. RUV-3 is not available!
## Warning in .mbecSetData(input.obj, new.cnts = new.cnts, type = type, label =
## label): Nothing to do here. Returning unchanged input.
mbec.obj <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj, model.vars = c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"),
                               method = "bmc", type = "clr")

mbec.obj <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj, model.vars = c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"),
                               method = "rbe", type = "clr")
mbec.obj <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj, model.vars = c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"),
                               method = "pn", type = "clr")
## Applying Percentile Normalization (PN).
## Warning in mbecPN(input.obj, model.vars, type = eval(type)): Batch variable is
## not a factor! Converting to factor now.
## Warning in mbecPN(input.obj, model.vars, type = eval(type)): Grouping variable
## is not a factor! Converting to factor now.
## Group Elevated is considered control group, i.e., reference
##           for normalization procedure. To change reference please 'relevel()'
##           grouping factor accordingly.
mbec.obj <- mbecCorrection(mbec.obj, model.vars = c("ExtractionDate",
                                                    "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"),
                               method = "svd", type = "clr")
## Applying Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) for batch-correction.

5. Post Report

 Provide the user with a comparative report that shows how the chosen batch effect correction algorithms changed the data-set compared to the initial values.
The parameter model.vars is a character vector with two elements. The first denotes the covariate column that describes the batch effect and the second one should be used for the presumed biological effect of interest, e.g., the group effect in case/control studies. The type parameter selects which abundance table is to be used “otu”, “clr”, “tss” .

mbecReportPost(input.obj=mbec.obj, 
               model.vars=c("ExtractionDate",
                            "Fibrinogen_InflamStat"), 
               type="clr")

## /usr/lib/rstudio-server/bin/quarto/bin/tools/x86_64/pandoc +RTS -K512m -RTS mbecReport_post.knit.md --to html4 --from markdown+autolink_bare_uris+tex_math_single_backslash --output /home/dpuerres/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/mbecReport_post.html --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/pagebreak.lua --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/latex-div.lua --lua-filter /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmarkdown/lua/table-classes.lua --embed-resources --standalone --variable bs3=TRUE --section-divs --table-of-contents --toc-depth 3 --variable toc_float=1 --variable toc_selectors=h1,h2,h3 --variable toc_collapsed=1 --variable toc_smooth_scroll=1 --variable toc_print=1 --template /opt/R/host-site-library/rmarkdown/rmd/h/default.html --highlight-style kate --variable theme=cerulean --mathjax --variable 'mathjax-url=https://mathjax.rstudio.com/latest/MathJax.js?config=TeX-AMS-MML_HTMLorMML' --include-in-header /tmp/RtmpbO9juX/rmarkdown-str2924173b5063d.html

6. Retrieve corrrected data

Because the MbecData class extends the phyloseq class, all functions from phyloseq can be used as well. They do however only apply to the otu_table slot and will return an object of class phyloseq, i.e., any transformations or corrections will be lost. To retrieve an object of class phyloseq that contains the otu_table of corrected counts, for downstream analyses, the user can employ the mbecGetPhyloseq() function. As before, the arguments type and label are used to specify which abundance table should be used in the returned object.

# To retrieve the CLR transformed counts, set type accordingly.
ps.clr <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="clr")
saveRDS(ps.clr, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/ps_clr.rds")

# corrected counts 
ps.bmc <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="cor", label="bmc")
saveRDS(ps.bmc, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/ps_bmc.rds")
ps.bat <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="cor", label="bat")
saveRDS(ps.bat, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/ps_bat.rds")
ps.rbe <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="cor", label="rbe")
saveRDS(ps.rbe, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/ps_rbe.rds")
ps.pn <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="cor", label="pn")
saveRDS(ps.pn, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/ps_pn.rds")
ps.svd <- mbecGetPhyloseq(mbec.obj, type="cor", label="svd")
saveRDS(ps.svd, file = "~/Cow_Inflammation_Study/Confounders/Scripts/MBECS_Fibrinogen/ps_svd.rds")
